topline: India dominates the day from sweeping financial reforms and RBI restructuring plans to Apple's antitrust clash and fuel deregulation, while Ireland upset cricket powerhouse India in T20, Pakistan's cross-border strikes on Afghanistan kill 35, and a US-Iran ceasefire deal offers fragile Middle East relief.

stories.headline: Ireland Stun India to Claim T20 Series Win
stories.what: In a major upset, Ireland defeated India in the T20 series, stunning the reigning world champions on home soil. The hosts claimed the series win, marking a historic moment for Irish cricket. The result is a rare and significant defeat for India in the shortest format, coming against a side that has historically been considered a minnow in world cricket. The win underlines Ireland's growing competitiveness on the international stage.
stories.lens: India's T20 world champions tag takes a dent from an unlikely Irish side — a reminder that no opponent can be taken lightly in the shortest format.
stories.article:
Ireland have pulled off one of their greatest cricketing achievements, defeating India to claim a T20 series win against the reigning world champions. The hosts stunned a full-strength Indian side on Irish soil, securing a result that will be talked about for years in both countries' cricket histories.

For Ireland, this represents the culmination of years of patient development as a full ICC member nation. Having long been categorised as an emerging side, defeating the dominant force in global T20 cricket is a watershed moment — validating investments in domestic infrastructure and player development that have quietly been bearing fruit.

For India, the series loss will come as a sharp wake-up call. The team, which lifted the T20 World Cup, now finds itself on the wrong end of a result that few could have predicted. Questions will inevitably arise about team selection, preparation, and whether the touring squad fully respected the challenge that Ireland now reliably poses.

The result sends a broader message to world cricket: Ireland are no longer just plucky competitors making up the numbers. They are capable of beating the best, and on a good day on their home pitches, they can outplay even the most decorated opposition. Indian team management will face tough scrutiny in the days ahead.
key_facts: Ireland defeated India to win the T20 series
India are the reigning T20 World Champions
Result described as a stunning upset by the hosts
Match played on Irish home soil

stories.headline: India Lifts Wartime Petrol and Diesel Sale Restrictions from July 1
stories.what: The Indian government has announced it will lift restrictions on the sale of petrol and diesel from July 1, as concerns over fuel supply ease. The curbs, which were imposed as a wartime measure amid heightened regional tensions, will now be withdrawn, signalling a return to normalcy in fuel distribution across the country. The move is expected to bring relief to consumers, retailers, and transport operators who have faced constraints on purchases in recent weeks.
stories.lens: Petrol and diesel restrictions being lifted from July 1 means everyday travel, trucking, and small businesses will stop facing fuel purchase limits — directly affecting millions of Indians.
stories.article:
India will end its wartime restrictions on the sale of petrol and diesel starting July 1, the government announced, as supply-side concerns that had prompted the curbs steadily ease. The decision signals an important step toward normalising the country's fuel economy after a period of tightly managed distribution.

The restrictions had been introduced in the backdrop of the India-Pakistan military confrontation, with authorities opting for controlled fuel sales to prevent hoarding and ensure supplies remained available for essential and defence-related needs. With tensions having de-escalated, the rationale for maintaining those curbs has weakened considerably.

For ordinary Indians, the lifting of these restrictions will have immediate practical impact. Motorists, truck drivers, farmers relying on diesel for irrigation, and small businesses that depend on fuel-powered equipment will no longer face purchase limits at petrol pumps. The relief will ripple through supply chains and logistics costs in the coming days.

The government's move is also a confidence signal — an implicit statement that the worst of the supply disruption is behind the country. Oil marketing companies are expected to restore normal retail operations from the start of July, with pump operators across states being informed of the rollback ahead of the deadline.
key_facts: Petrol and diesel sale restrictions lifted from July 1
Curbs were imposed as wartime measures amid India-Pakistan conflict
Government says supply concerns have eased
Normal fuel retail to resume across the country

stories.headline: US-Iran Agree to Halt Strikes, Talks Scheduled; Tehran Says No Technical Meeting Imminent
stories.what: The United States and Iran have agreed to halt strikes against each other and hold talks this week, a US official confirmed, even as Tehran pushed back by saying no technical meeting is expected in the coming days. President Trump separately announced a meeting with Iran in Qatar. The contradictory signals reflect ongoing fragility in the ceasefire arrangement, with both sides appearing to calibrate their public messaging carefully ahead of potential Doha-based negotiations.
stories.lens: A US-Iran ceasefire and talks directly affect oil prices, regional stability, and India's energy security — New Delhi watches closely given its dependence on West Asian oil and its sizeable diaspora in the Gulf.
stories.article:
The United States and Iran have reached an agreement to halt military strikes against each other and convene talks this week, according to a US official, in what would mark a significant pause in the West Asian conflict that has roiled global markets and regional diplomacy. President Donald Trump separately confirmed he would meet with Iranian representatives in Qatar.

However, Iran's own statements complicated the picture. Tehran declared that no technical meeting with the US is expected in the coming days, creating a contradictory public record that reflects how delicate — and potentially fragile — the ceasefire arrangement remains. Diplomats on both sides appeared to be managing domestic audiences as much as each other.

The backdrop is a period of sustained military skirmishes between US and Iranian forces and Iran-aligned groups in the region. A halt to strikes, even if temporary and contested in its details, would ease fears of a broader escalation that many analysts had warned could engulf the wider Middle East.

For India, developments in West Asia carry direct strategic weight. New Delhi is heavily dependent on Gulf oil imports, and a significant portion of the Indian diaspora lives and works in the region. Indian diplomacy has maintained careful neutrality throughout the conflict, and any movement toward talks will be quietly welcomed in South Block as reducing risks to energy supplies and the safety of Indian nationals abroad.
key_facts: US and Iran agreed to halt strikes and meet this week
Trump announced a meeting with Iran in Qatar
Iran said no technical meeting expected in coming days
Talks reportedly to be held in Doha

stories.headline: 5 Dead in Shooting in Germany, 2 Arrested
stories.what: A shooting in Germany has left five people dead, with two individuals arrested in connection with the attack. Details about the location and motive remain limited, but the incident marks one of Germany's more deadly firearms incidents in recent times. German authorities have launched an investigation, and the arrests suggest police have moved swiftly to detain suspects believed to be responsible for the killings.
stories.lens: A mass shooting in Germany — a country with strict gun laws — grabs global attention and raises fresh questions about public safety in Europe.
stories.article:
Five people were killed in a shooting in Germany, with authorities swiftly arresting two suspects in connection with the deadly attack. The incident represents a serious gun violence episode in a country that maintains among the strictest firearms regulations in Europe, making such events particularly striking when they occur.

German law enforcement moved rapidly, securing two arrests that suggest investigators had early leads on those responsible. The speed of the arrests may indicate the attack was not the work of unknown assailants, and investigators will now work to establish the motive, the relationship between the suspects and the victims, and whether there is any broader threat to public safety.

Mass shootings are relatively rare in Germany compared to some other countries, and each one prompts a national conversation about security, gun control, and social cohesion. Authorities across German states are expected to review security protocols in the wake of the incident.

The international community, including India, has noted the event. Five deaths in a single shooting incident in a peaceful European country underlines that no society is entirely immune to sudden, devastating violence, and the German government will face pressure to provide answers about how the attack unfolded and what can be done to prevent similar tragedies.
key_facts: 5 people killed in a shooting in Germany
2 suspects arrested in connection with the attack
Germany has strict firearms regulations
Investigation underway to establish motive

stories.headline: India Distances Itself from Track 2 Dialogue Reports with Pakistan
stories.what: India has officially distanced itself from reports of Track 2 dialogue with Pakistan, stating there is no official government involvement in any such back-channel talks. The clarification comes amid media reports suggesting that informal diplomatic conversations between the two countries may be underway, with the Indus Waters Treaty emerging as a new flashpoint amid speculation triggered by the Track 2 discussions. New Delhi's statement makes clear it does not endorse or sanction the reported talks.
stories.lens: Any hint of back-channel India-Pakistan diplomacy — even when officially denied — shapes public sentiment and political narratives in both countries, and signals how carefully New Delhi is managing post-conflict optics.
stories.article:
India has formally distanced itself from reports suggesting that Track 2 dialogue with Pakistan is underway, clarifying that there is no official government involvement in any such back-channel diplomatic engagement. The statement reflects New Delhi's determination to keep tight control over the narrative around any potential India-Pakistan thaw in the aftermath of their recent military confrontation.

Track 2 diplomacy — involving academics, former officials, think tanks, and civil society figures rather than governments directly — has historically served as a pressure valve and informal communication channel between the two neighbours. However, the Indian government's distancing signals that it is not ready to be seen as endorsing even informal conversations at this politically sensitive juncture.

The Indus Waters Treaty has emerged as a new point of tension amid the speculation, with reports indicating that water-sharing concerns were among the issues discussed in the alleged informal talks. The treaty, which had long been considered one of the most durable elements of the India-Pakistan relationship even through wars, has come under fresh pressure following the recent military conflict.

Politically, New Delhi's clarification is aimed both at a domestic audience that remains hostile to the idea of engagement with Islamabad, and at Pakistan, where the reports may have created expectations of a diplomatic opening. The government appears to be signalling that any return to formal or semi-formal dialogue will happen only on India's terms and timeline.
key_facts: India denies any official involvement in Track 2 talks with Pakistan
Indus Waters Treaty identified as a new flashpoint in bilateral tensions
Reports of informal dialogue triggered official Indian clarification
India-Pakistan relations remain fraught after recent military conflict

stories.headline: South Korea eyes 'second wave' of investment in India, targeting shipbuilding and defence
stories.what: South Korea is actively planning a 'second wave' of investment in India, with a strategic focus on the shipbuilding and defence sectors, according to Moneycontrol. The move reflects growing South Korean interest in India as a manufacturing and strategic partner, particularly as India ramps up its defence modernisation programme and pushes for domestic shipbuilding capacity under its maritime development goals. South Korean firms have existing investments in India's automotive and electronics sectors, and the new focus areas represent a significant expansion into strategic industries.
stories.lens: South Korean investment in Indian shipbuilding and defence could accelerate India's military self-reliance ambitions and create skilled manufacturing jobs in a sector the government has been trying hard to grow domestically.
stories.article:
South Korea is positioning itself for a 'second wave' of investment in India, with shipbuilding and defence emerging as the priority sectors for the next phase of bilateral economic engagement, Moneycontrol has reported. The development signals that South Korean strategic and commercial interest in India is moving well beyond the automotive and consumer electronics domains where Korean brands are already well established.

India's defence sector has been undergoing rapid transformation, with the government pushing hard for indigenisation through the 'Make in India' programme and raising foreign direct investment limits in defence manufacturing. The country has also set ambitious targets for domestic shipbuilding as part of its maritime vision, creating opportunities for foreign partners with established expertise in both domains — exactly the areas where South Korea excels globally.

South Korean shipbuilders and defence firms bring world-class technological capabilities and could contribute meaningfully to India's stated goal of reducing import dependency in critical sectors. A partnership model involving technology transfer and joint ventures would align well with Indian policy frameworks that incentivise domestic production over simple imports.

For India, Korean interest in these strategic industries represents a broadening of the country's network of defence and industrial partners beyond the traditional suppliers of Russia, France, and the United States. As South Korea positions itself as a growing defence exporter globally, India — with its scale and appetite for modernisation — is a natural and high-value market for this 'second wave' of Korean investment ambition.
key_facts: South Korea planning 'second wave' of investment in India
Focus sectors: shipbuilding and defence
Existing Korean investments in India span automotive and electronics

stories.headline: Cabinet commits Rs 30,000 crore additional investment to NIIF to boost infrastructure
stories.what: The Union Cabinet has approved an additional investment commitment of Rs 30,000 crore in the National Investment and Infrastructure Fund (NIIF), a significant move aimed at accelerating infrastructure spending across India. The infusion is designed to catalyse institutional capital into the country's infrastructure sector, amplifying the fund's ability to attract co-investment from domestic and global institutional investors. NIIF functions as India's quasi-sovereign wealth fund, channelling long-term capital into roads, ports, energy, and logistics projects.
stories.lens: A Rs 30,000 crore government commitment to NIIF could directly translate into faster highways, ports, and energy projects — infrastructure that affects jobs and everyday costs for millions of Indians.
stories.article:
The Union Cabinet has approved an additional investment commitment of Rs 30,000 crore into the National Investment and Infrastructure Fund, signalling a strong push to fast-track infrastructure development across the country. The decision, announced via the Press Information Bureau, aims to both deepen NIIF's firepower and draw in larger pools of institutional capital from sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, and other long-term investors.

NIIF was set up by the Indian government as a quasi-sovereign fund to invest in infrastructure and allied sectors. The fresh commitment substantially strengthens the government's anchor position in the fund, which is typically used to crowd in private and international co-investment at multiples of the government's own stake. This model has previously attracted investors from Abu Dhabi, Canada, and other major capital-exporting nations.

The timing of the decision is significant. India is in the midst of an ambitious multi-year infrastructure build-out, with projects spanning highways, ports, renewable energy, and urban transit. Channelling additional capital through NIIF gives the government a mechanism to deploy funds commercially and efficiently, rather than solely through budget expenditure, reducing fiscal pressure while maintaining strategic control.

For India's broader economy, the move is a signal that capital formation in hard infrastructure remains a top policy priority even as global uncertainty weighs on foreign investment flows. The Cabinet's decision is expected to be welcomed by infrastructure developers, project financiers, and institutional investors watching India's long-term growth story.
key_facts: Cabinet approves Rs 30,000 crore additional commitment to NIIF
Funds aimed at accelerating infrastructure investment in India
NIIF designed to catalyse institutional capital alongside government funds

stories.headline: Apple accuses India's antitrust body of 'copy-pasting' rivals' claims in its investigation
stories.what: Apple has accused India's Competition Commission of India (CCI) of 'copy-pasting' claims made by rivals into its antitrust investigation against the tech giant, in an exclusive report by Reuters. The allegation suggests Apple believes the CCI's case is not independently constructed but has been substantially shaped by the complaints of competing firms. The dispute marks a significant flashpoint in India's increasingly assertive regulatory scrutiny of Big Tech, as the CCI has ramped up investigations into global technology companies in recent years.
stories.lens: How India's antitrust regulator handles a pushback from one of the world's most powerful companies will shape the rules governing how Big Tech operates in one of its fastest-growing markets.
stories.article:
Apple has fired back at India's Competition Commission, accusing the regulator of 'copy-pasting' claims made by its rivals into the formal antitrust investigation against the iPhone maker, according to an exclusive Reuters report. The allegation is a pointed legal and reputational challenge to the CCI, implying that the investigation lacks independent rigour and has been unduly influenced by Apple's competitors.

The CCI has been stepping up its oversight of global technology platforms in recent years, launching probes into companies including Google and now Apple over alleged anti-competitive practices. Apple's pushback signals that the company intends to contest the investigation aggressively rather than negotiate a settlement, which could set up a prolonged legal battle.

The charge of 'copy-pasting' rivals' submissions, if substantiated, would raise procedural questions about the integrity of the CCI's investigative process. Regulators globally have faced criticism for leaning too heavily on complainants' narratives, and Apple appears to be deploying a similar defence strategy to that used in other jurisdictions where it has faced antitrust scrutiny.

The outcome of this dispute will be closely watched by multinational technology companies operating in India. A robust CCI ruling upheld against Apple would embolden the regulator; a finding in Apple's favour could prompt the CCI to tighten its investigative procedures. Either way, the case underlines that India's regulatory environment for Big Tech is growing significantly more complex.
key_facts: Apple accuses India's CCI of copying rivals' claims in antitrust probe
Allegation reported exclusively by Reuters
CCI has increasingly targeted global Big Tech firms in India

stories.headline: India plotting sweeping financial reform that could reshape RBI, banks, corporates and investors
stories.what: India is working on a major financial sector reform that could significantly alter the operating landscape for the Reserve Bank of India, commercial banks, corporate borrowers, and investors, according to The Economic Times. The details of the proposed changes remain in development, but the breadth of stakeholders named — the central bank, banks, corporates, and investors — suggests a systemic overhaul rather than an incremental policy tweak. Such a reform, if enacted, would mark one of the most consequential shifts in India's financial architecture in years.
stories.lens: Any reform touching the RBI, banks, and corporates simultaneously has the potential to change borrowing costs, credit availability, and investment returns for ordinary Indians and businesses alike.
stories.article:
India is reportedly working on a landmark financial sector reform that could reshape the relationship between the Reserve Bank of India, commercial banks, corporate entities, and the broader investor community, The Economic Times has reported. The scale of the proposed changes, described as a 'game-changer', suggests the government is contemplating structural rather than cosmetic reforms to the financial system.

The precise contours of the reform have not been fully detailed publicly, but the involvement of the RBI points to possible changes in monetary policy frameworks, bank regulation, or the rules governing how banks lend to and interact with corporate India. Reforms of this breadth could touch capital requirements, credit markets, corporate bond markets, or the broader framework of financial intermediation.

India's financial sector has long been seen as a constraint on the economy's growth potential, with banks burdened by legacy non-performing assets, corporate bond markets underdeveloped relative to the economy's size, and regulatory complexity creating friction for investors. A sweeping reform effort would address longstanding structural issues that have held back deeper capital market development.

Market participants and policy watchers will be closely monitoring further developments. If the reform moves from planning to implementation, it could catalyse significant shifts in how capital is allocated across the Indian economy — with implications for everything from home loan rates to equity valuations and foreign investor appetite.
key_facts: India planning major financial reform affecting RBI, banks, and corporates
Reform described as a 'game-changer' by The Economic Times
Could reshape credit markets, investment frameworks, and banking regulation

stories.headline: Prosus India posts $781 million revenue in FY26, turns adjusted EBITDA positive
stories.what: Prosus, the Dutch technology investment giant, has reported that its India business posted revenue of $781 million in FY26 and crossed into adjusted EBITDA profitability for the first time, according to Entrackr. The Indian portfolio of Prosus includes major consumer internet assets, most notably Swiggy and PayU, which have very different financial profiles — PayU generating profits while Swiggy continues to burn cash. The milestone of adjusted EBITDA positivity marks a turning point for one of the largest foreign-backed tech portfolios in the country.
stories.lens: Prosus turning EBITDA-positive in India signals that the era of bottomless startup losses may be giving way to a new phase of sustainable growth — a shift that affects investor confidence across the entire Indian tech ecosystem.
stories.article:
Prosus's India business has reported revenue of $781 million in FY26 and achieved adjusted EBITDA profitability, marking a significant milestone for the Dutch tech investment conglomerate's largest emerging-market bet, according to Entrackr. The result reflects years of pressure on Prosus-backed companies to move toward financial sustainability after a prolonged period of growth-at-all-costs investment.

The India portfolio, however, tells two starkly contrasting stories. PayU, Prosus's payments arm, is profitable and generating healthy returns, while Swiggy, the food and quick-commerce delivery platform, continues to burn cash at scale. The aggregate EBITDA positivity is therefore a blended outcome, with PayU's profits outweighing Swiggy's ongoing losses at the adjusted level.

For Swiggy, which listed on Indian stock exchanges in late 2024, the continued cash burn is a concern for public market shareholders who are watching closely for a credible path to profitability. The company competes fiercely with Zomato in food delivery and with Blinkit and Zepto in quick commerce — a three-way battle that has kept marketing and logistics costs elevated across the industry.

The FY26 results position Prosus's India portfolio as a maturing asset base even if parts of it remain loss-making. For the broader Indian startup ecosystem, the Prosus numbers serve as a data point in the ongoing narrative shift from valuation-led growth to profitability-led resilience — a theme that is now central to how both investors and founders think about building companies in India.
key_facts: Prosus India revenue: $781 million in FY26
Business turns adjusted EBITDA positive for the first time
PayU is profitable; Swiggy continues to burn cash

stories.headline: Principal Scientific Adviser launches TRL Compass Platform and signs MoU with DSCI to boost deep-tech innovation
stories.what: The Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser to the Government of India launched the TRL Compass Platform and signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the Data Security Council of India (DSCI). The TRL Compass is designed to help map and track the Technology Readiness Level of innovations across India's research and startup ecosystem. The MoU with DSCI signals a formal partnership aimed at strengthening data security standards alongside deep-tech development.
stories.lens: For Indian researchers, startups, and technology policymakers, TRL Compass could become a key tool for identifying which homegrown innovations are ready to scale — directly influencing funding and government procurement decisions.
stories.article:
The Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser (PSA) to the Government of India launched the TRL Compass Platform in a move aimed at systematically tracking how close Indian innovations are to real-world deployment. Simultaneously, the PSA's office signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the Data Security Council of India (DSCI), formalising collaboration between the country's top scientific advisory body and its leading data security industry group.

TRL — or Technology Readiness Level — is an internationally recognised framework used to assess how mature a technology is, from basic research all the way to fully deployed product. The TRL Compass Platform is intended to give policymakers, investors, and researchers a structured view of where Indian innovations sit on this spectrum, helping direct funding and support to technologies that are close to commercialisation.

The MoU with DSCI adds a data security dimension to this effort, recognising that as Indian deep-tech scales up, robust data governance and cybersecurity practices must be embedded from early stages. DSCI, which works closely with both government and industry on data protection standards, is well-positioned to support startups navigating India's evolving regulatory landscape.

The launch reflects the government's broader ambition to bridge the gap between India's strong research base and its relatively lower rate of technology commercialisation. By creating a transparent readiness map, TRL Compass could help align government procurement, venture funding, and academic research priorities — giving promising but underfunded innovations a clearer pathway to market.
key_facts: TRL Compass Platform launched by Office of Principal Scientific Adviser
MoU signed between PSA's office and Data Security Council of India (DSCI)
Platform tracks Technology Readiness Levels of Indian innovations

stories.headline: Rs 91,500 crore and 2.45 lakh jobs at risk as digital rules squeeze India's startup ecosystem
stories.what: A new report warns that India's startup ecosystem faces severe stress from tightening digital regulations, with as much as Rs 91,500 crore in value and 2.45 lakh jobs potentially under threat. The findings, cited by The Economic Times, signal that proposed and existing digital governance rules are creating deep uncertainty for founders and investors alike. The scale of potential damage puts pressure on policymakers to recalibrate regulation without stifling innovation.
stories.lens: For anyone working in or investing in Indian startups, these figures represent real livelihoods and economic momentum that could be lost if digital rules are not carefully designed.
stories.article:
India's startup ecosystem — one of the largest in the world — faces a potential blow of Rs 91,500 crore in economic value and as many as 2.45 lakh jobs, according to a report flagged by The Economic Times. The warning comes as a wave of digital regulations, spanning data governance, platform liability, and compliance mandates, creates mounting uncertainty for businesses of all sizes.

The report's findings are particularly striking because they put precise numbers to a concern that founders and venture capitalists have been voicing for months: that well-intentioned regulation, poorly timed or designed, can choke off the very innovation India needs to compete globally. Startups, especially early-stage and growth-phase companies, often lack the legal and compliance infrastructure to absorb rapidly shifting regulatory demands.

A separate Mint survey published the same day reinforced this anxiety, finding that data governance — not artificial intelligence — is the single biggest regulatory worry among Indian startups. Together, the two reports paint a picture of an ecosystem that feels squeezed from multiple directions at once, with founders prioritising regulatory navigation over product development.

The stakes are high for the broader Indian economy. The startup sector has been a key driver of tech employment and foreign investment over the past decade. If the report's projections materialise, the damage would ripple beyond founders and venture funds into salaries, vendor ecosystems, and consumer services that millions of Indians use daily. Policymakers now face the challenge of balancing legitimate concerns around data security and platform accountability with the need to keep India's entrepreneurial engine running.
key_facts: Rs 91,500 crore in startup value potentially at risk
2.45 lakh jobs could be lost due to digital regulations
Data governance — not AI — is startups' top regulatory concern (Mint survey)

stories.headline: Data governance, not AI, is India's startups' biggest regulatory fear, survey finds
stories.what: A survey reported by Mint reveals that Indian startups are more anxious about data governance rules than about artificial intelligence regulation — a counterintuitive finding given the global focus on AI risk. Founders and executives point to overlapping and ambiguous data protection requirements as their most immediate compliance burden. The survey adds nuance to the ongoing debate about how India should regulate its fast-growing digital economy.
stories.lens: For Indian entrepreneurs and tech workers, this signals that the data protection framework under development could reshape how startups operate and hire — well before any AI law arrives.
stories.article:
Indian startups are more worried about data governance than artificial intelligence regulation, according to a survey published by Mint. The finding cuts against the prevailing global narrative that AI is the defining regulatory challenge of the moment — for India's startup community, the more immediate threat lies in how the government handles data protection, privacy rules, and platform compliance obligations.

The survey captures a mood of regulatory fatigue among founders, many of whom say they are spending increasing time and resources on legal compliance rather than product development. Data governance concerns span a wide range of issues: how user data can be stored and transferred, what consents are required, and how liability is assigned when data is mishandled.

The timing matters. India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act has been passed but its rules are still being finalised, leaving businesses in a prolonged state of uncertainty. Startups, particularly those handling health, financial, or consumer data, say they cannot make long-term infrastructure or hiring decisions until the regulatory picture clarifies.

Read alongside a separate report warning that Rs 91,500 crore in startup value and 2.45 lakh jobs are at risk from digital rules, the Mint survey suggests that the regulatory environment has become a genuine drag on India's startup ambitions. Investors and industry bodies are now calling on the government to publish clear implementation timelines and provide sandbox environments where startups can test compliance approaches without existential legal risk.
key_facts: Data governance ranked as top regulatory concern, above AI
India's DPDP Act rules still being finalised, creating uncertainty
Survey published by Mint covers Indian startup founders and executives

stories.headline: Bengaluru techie earning ₹45 lakh a year says he has 'nothing left' by month-end
stories.what: A Bengaluru-based technology professional earning ₹45 lakh annually has shared that he feels completely broke by the end of every month, triggering a viral debate on social media about the real cost of living in India's tech capital. The post has resonated widely, with many professionals in similar income brackets sharing their own experiences of lifestyle inflation, high rents, loan EMIs, and family obligations eating into what looks like a generous salary on paper.
stories.lens: For millions of salaried Indians in metro cities, this story puts a number to a quiet anxiety — that high incomes no longer guarantee financial comfort when city costs, EMIs, and social pressures compound.
stories.article:
A software professional based in Bengaluru earning ₹45 lakh a year has ignited a wide online conversation after admitting he has virtually nothing left by the time each month ends. The post, picked up by The Times of India, quickly went viral, with thousands of responses from tech workers across the country who said the situation felt all too familiar.

The complaint points to a structural reality of life in India's major tech hubs: salaries that look impressive in isolation are often eroded by a combination of high rents, home loan EMIs, car loans, private school fees, and the social expectations that come with a certain professional tier. In Bengaluru in particular, the cost of housing has risen sharply over the past few years, with many professionals spending a substantial share of their take-home pay on accommodation alone.

The internet's reaction was divided. Some users sympathised, arguing that the cost of living in India's tier-one cities has genuinely outpaced salary growth for many roles. Others were blunter, pointing out that ₹45 lakh puts the individual comfortably in the top few percent of Indian earners, and that the problem lies with spending choices rather than income levels. Financial advisers weighed in with observations about lifestyle inflation — the tendency for expenses to expand in lockstep with earnings.

The episode reflects a broader anxiety among India's urban professional class: the gap between headline salary figures and actual financial security is wider than many expected. As housing costs, education expenses, and consumer aspirations all rise together, the old assumption that a high-paying tech job guarantees stability is increasingly being questioned in cities like Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Hyderabad.
key_facts: Bengaluru tech professional earns ₹45 lakh per year
Reports having no money left by month-end
Story sparked viral debate about lifestyle inflation in metro cities

stories.headline: Sold a company at 16, raised $3 million at 19: How Dhravya Shah built AI startup Supermemory
stories.what: Dhravya Shah, an Indian-origin entrepreneur, sold his first company at the age of 16, raised $3 million at 19, and secured the prestigious US O-1 visa — granted to individuals of extraordinary ability — all before most of his peers had finished college. Shah's venture is an AI startup called Supermemory, which he built while still a teenager. His story, reported by The Times of India, has sparked wide discussion about India's young builder culture and the paths open to exceptionally driven founders.
stories.lens: Shah's journey is a concrete example of how young Indians are competing at the highest global level in AI entrepreneurship — and a signal of the talent pipeline India is producing for the world stage.
stories.article:
Dhravya Shah has done what most entrepreneurs spend decades trying to achieve — and he did it before turning 20. The Indian-origin founder sold a company at 16, raised $3 million at 19, and earned a US O-1 visa, a category reserved for individuals who demonstrate extraordinary ability in their field. His current venture is Supermemory, an AI startup that has drawn significant attention in global tech circles.

Shah's trajectory reflects a broader shift in how young Indians are engaging with technology entrepreneurship. Where earlier generations often pursued traditional engineering or management careers before attempting a startup, a new cohort is building and selling companies in their mid-teens, leveraging open-source tools, global online communities, and remote-first investor networks to compress the startup timeline dramatically.

The O-1 visa recognition is particularly noteworthy. The US immigration category is notoriously difficult to obtain, typically requiring evidence of sustained national or international acclaim — making Shah's receipt of it at 19 an unusual achievement that underscores how seriously American institutions are taking young global tech talent.

Shah's story has gone viral in Indian tech communities, reigniting debate about whether India's education system adequately nurtures or, alternatively, inadvertently suppresses this kind of unconventional ambition. For every Dhravya Shah who breaks through, critics argue, there are thousands of equally capable young Indians whose entrepreneurial instincts are never given room to develop. His success, reported by The Times of India, is as much a conversation-starter about India's talent ecosystem as it is a personal achievement.
key_facts: Dhravya Shah sold a company at age 16
Raised $3 million at age 19
Received US O-1 visa for extraordinary ability
Founded AI startup Supermemory

stories.headline: PM Modi Addresses Seychelles National Assembly, Attends 50th Anniversary of Independence
stories.what: Prime Minister Narendra Modi addressed the Seychelles National Assembly during a state visit, reiterating India's role as the island nation's 'trusted partner'. Modi also attended celebrations marking the 50th anniversary of Seychelles' independence and met members of the Indian diaspora there. The visit underscores India's deepening engagement with island nations in the Indian Ocean Region as part of its 'Neighbourhood First' and maritime diplomacy agenda.
stories.lens: India's outreach to Seychelles reflects its broader strategic push to strengthen ties with Indian Ocean island nations, a region where China has also been expanding its influence.
stories.article:
Prime Minister Narendra Modi addressed the National Assembly of Seychelles during an official state visit, describing India as the island nation's 'trusted partner' and reaffirming bilateral ties that span development cooperation, security and people-to-people links. The address to the legislature is a mark of the high level of diplomatic engagement India is seeking with the archipelago.

Modi also attended the celebrations marking the 50th anniversary of Seychelles' independence — a milestone occasion that provided a high-profile platform for India to demonstrate its commitment to the relationship. He separately met members of the Indian diaspora in Seychelles, a community that has historically played a bridge-building role between the two countries.

The visit fits into India's broader Indian Ocean strategy, which prioritises close ties with island nations in the region. Seychelles holds strategic importance given its location in the western Indian Ocean and has been a partner in India's maritime security and humanitarian assistance frameworks.

The engagement comes at a time when major powers — including China — are competing for influence across Indian Ocean island states through infrastructure investment and development finance. India's emphasis on being a 'trusted partner', as opposed to a transactional investor, is a deliberate diplomatic positioning that New Delhi has cultivated across the region.
key_facts: PM Modi addressed the Seychelles National Assembly
India described as Seychelles' 'trusted partner'
Modi attended Seychelles' 50th independence anniversary celebrations
Modi met Indian diaspora members in Seychelles

stories.headline: Yasin Malik Named Key Accused in 1990 Sarla Bhat Rape-Murder Case, Chargesheet Filed After 36 Years
stories.what: Kashmir's State Investigation Agency has filed a 737-page chargesheet in the 1990 rape and murder of Sarla Bhat, a Kashmiri Pandit nurse, naming JKLF chief Yasin Malik as the main accused along with four other JKLF operatives. The case, one of the most emblematic atrocities of the Kashmir terror era, has reached court 36 years after the killing. The chargesheet includes witness accounts and references an interview with Bitta Karate, another notorious figure from that period.
stories.lens: The filing marks a rare moment of legal accountability for crimes committed against Kashmiri Pandits during the 1990 exodus, directly affecting the community's long quest for justice.
stories.article:
Thirty-six years after the rape and murder of Sarla Bhat, a Kashmiri Pandit nurse whose killing became a landmark case of Kashmir's terror era, the State Investigation Agency has filed a 737-page chargesheet naming JKLF chief Yasin Malik as the main accused. Four other JKLF operatives are also named in the document, which arrives as Malik is already serving a life sentence in a separate terror-funding case.

Sarla Bhat was raped and killed in 1990, branded an 'informant' by militants in what investigators say was a targeted act of terror against the Kashmiri Pandit community during the violence that drove hundreds of thousands from the Valley. The SIA chargesheet draws on witness testimony and includes details from an interview with Bitta Karate, a name long associated with killings from that period.

The case had remained unresolved for decades, making it one of the most painful symbols of unpunished violence from the Kashmir insurgency. The decision to file a formal chargesheet now signals the government's intent to pursue accountability for crimes against Kashmiri Pandits, many of whom have waited over three decades for legal redress.

With Yasin Malik already in custody, the chargesheet opens the path for formal trial proceedings. Legal observers note that successfully prosecuting a decades-old case of this nature will depend heavily on the strength of witness accounts and forensic documentation assembled in the 737-page document.
key_facts: SIA files 737-page chargesheet in 1990 Sarla Bhat rape-murder case
Yasin Malik named main accused; four JKLF operatives also named
Sarla Bhat was a Kashmiri Pandit nurse killed in 1990
Case reaches court 36 years after the killing
Chargesheet includes witness accounts and Bitta Karate interview

stories.headline: Delhi Cabinet Clears New EV Policy: Electric Autos Only From 2027, E-Two-Wheelers From 2028
stories.what: The Delhi cabinet has approved a new electric vehicle policy that mandates a phased transition away from fossil-fuel-powered vehicles: only electric auto-rickshaws will be permitted from 2027, and all new two-wheelers registered in Delhi must be electric from 2028. The policy signals a significant shift in how the capital's vast fleet of last-mile and personal vehicles will be powered in the coming years, with direct implications for auto drivers, two-wheeler buyers and the city's chronic air pollution.
stories.lens: For millions of Delhiites — from daily auto commuters to two-wheeler buyers — this policy sets firm deadlines that will reshape how they travel and what vehicles they can buy.
stories.article:
The Delhi cabinet has approved a sweeping new electric vehicle policy that sets hard deadlines for phasing out petrol and CNG-powered vehicles from two of the city's most common vehicle categories. Under the policy, only electric auto-rickshaws will be allowed to operate in Delhi from 2027, while all new two-wheelers registered in the capital must be electric from 2028.

The move targets vehicle segments that collectively account for a significant share of Delhi's traffic and emissions. Auto-rickshaws are a primary mode of last-mile connectivity for millions of commuters, while two-wheelers dominate private mobility across the city. Transitioning both to electric power is expected to deliver a measurable improvement in Delhi's notoriously poor air quality.

The policy comes as Delhi grapples with some of the worst air pollution indices among major global cities, with vehicle exhaust a leading contributor. By setting legally binding timelines rather than voluntary targets, the cabinet is signalling a more coercive approach than previous clean-air initiatives.

For auto drivers, many of whom operate on thin margins, the shift will require access to affordable EV financing and a reliable charging network — infrastructure gaps the government will need to address in the run-up to the 2027 deadline. Two-wheeler manufacturers are already accelerating their electric lineups in anticipation of tightening regulations across Indian states.
key_facts: Only electric auto-rickshaws permitted in Delhi from 2027
All new two-wheelers in Delhi must be electric from 2028
Policy approved by Delhi cabinet

stories.headline: CBSE Relaxes Three-Language Policy for Classes 7-9, Allows Two Foreign Language Options
stories.what: The Central Board of Secondary Education has issued guidelines relaxing the implementation of the three-language policy for students in Classes 7, 8 and 9, allowing two foreign language options as part of the framework. The move comes with significant pushback from some states — Karnataka's school body has objected to Kannada being designated as the third language, highlighting the political sensitivity of language policy in education. The policy also carries a major update for Class 10 students.
stories.lens: CBSE's language policy directly affects crores of school students and their families, and the state-level resistance signals that the national rollout could face significant friction.
stories.article:
The Central Board of Secondary Education has notified detailed guidelines for the three-language policy applicable to students in Classes 7, 8 and 9, relaxing earlier provisions to allow two foreign language options within the framework. The circular represents the board's attempt to balance the National Education Policy's language mandate with the practical diversity of its affiliated schools across India.

The policy has immediately run into political resistance in Karnataka, where the state school education body has objected to Kannada being positioned as the third language under the CBSE structure. The objection reflects a long-standing tension between national curriculum frameworks and states' assertions of linguistic identity — a fault line that has repeatedly complicated education policy in southern India.

The guidelines also carry a significant update for Class 10 students, though the specific details of those provisions add nuance to how the policy will be applied at the board examination level. Schools affiliated to CBSE across the country will now need to align their language offerings with the new framework.

The three-language formula has been a contested element of Indian education policy for decades, with disagreements over which languages should be included and in what order. The CBSE's latest notification is likely to trigger further debate between the Centre and state governments, particularly in non-Hindi-speaking states where the formula is viewed with suspicion.
key_facts: CBSE relaxes three-language policy for Classes 7, 8 and 9
Two foreign language options now allowed under the framework
Karnataka school body opposes Kannada as third language under CBSE
Policy includes a major update for Class 10 students

stories.headline: Pune Court Sentences 65-Year-Old to Death for Rape and Murder of 3-Year-Old Girl, Calls it 'Rarest of Rare'
stories.what: A Pune court has sentenced a 65-year-old man to death for the rape and murder of a 3-year-old girl, invoking the 'rarest of rare' doctrine that Indian courts apply to justify capital punishment. The case involves the most extreme form of violence against a child and has drawn attention for both the brutality of the crime and the age of the convict. The verdict reflects judicial resolve to award the maximum penalty in cases of child sexual violence resulting in death.
stories.lens: The death sentence in this case underscores the courts' application of capital punishment for child rape-murder — a policy debate that directly concerns child safety across India.
stories.article:
A Pune court has awarded the death penalty to a 65-year-old man convicted of raping and murdering a 3-year-old girl, declaring the case to fall in the 'rarest of rare' category that justifies capital punishment under Indian law. The verdict, delivered on Monday, is among the starkest sentences handed down in recent months for crimes against children.

The 'rarest of rare' doctrine, established by the Supreme Court of India, requires courts to demonstrate that no alternative punishment would suffice before imposing death. In this instance, the court found that the extreme vulnerability of the victim, the heinous nature of the assault, and the betrayal of the child's safety met that threshold.

The case is likely to fuel ongoing debate around child protection laws and the effectiveness of stringent sentencing as a deterrent. India strengthened its laws on child sexual abuse in 2019, introducing the death penalty as an option for aggravated offences under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act.

The convict, at 65, is among the older individuals to receive such a sentence in a child rape-murder case. Legal experts note that the sentence is subject to confirmation by a higher court, as is mandatory in all capital cases under Indian criminal procedure.
key_facts: Pune court sentences 65-year-old man to death
Convicted of rape and murder of a 3-year-old girl
Court invoked the 'rarest of rare' doctrine for capital punishment

stories.headline: China Develops AI Cybersecurity Tool Said to Match Anthropic's Mythos
stories.what: China has developed a new artificial intelligence-based cybersecurity tool that researchers describe as being on par with Anthropic's Mythos model in capability, according to NDTV. The development marks a significant milestone in China's effort to build frontier AI systems independent of Western technology, and in a domain — cybersecurity — that carries acute strategic implications. The tool's emergence adds a new dimension to the global AI competition, particularly as the US has been tightening export controls on advanced AI to China.
stories.lens: A Chinese AI cybersecurity tool matching Western frontier models raises real concerns about the global AI arms race and has direct implications for India's own digital security landscape.
stories.article:
China has developed an AI-powered cybersecurity tool that is being assessed as comparable in capability to Anthropic's Mythos model, marking a notable moment in the country's drive to achieve parity with leading Western AI systems. NDTV reported the development, which signals that China's AI research community is closing the gap with frontier Western models even in technically demanding, high-stakes domains like cybersecurity.

The timing is striking: Anthropic had only recently resumed a limited rollout of Mythos 5 following US export restrictions, and China's parallel development of a comparable tool underscores why those restrictions exist in the first place. The cybersecurity domain is particularly sensitive because AI tools in this space can be used both defensively — to detect and respond to threats — and offensively, to identify and exploit vulnerabilities.

For India, which shares complex relationships with both the United States and China on technology and security matters, the emergence of a Chinese AI cybersecurity tool of this calibre is relevant on multiple levels. India's government networks, critical infrastructure, and private sector have all faced cyber threats with documented links to state-sponsored actors, making advances in adversarial AI a topic of genuine national security interest.

The development also reinforces the broader narrative that the global AI race is no longer a two-horse contest between a handful of US labs. China's ability to produce frontier-class AI in sensitive domains will likely intensify calls — in India and elsewhere — for domestic AI capability building rather than reliance on any single foreign ecosystem.
key_facts: China has built an AI cybersecurity tool described as on par with Anthropic's Mythos
The tool operates in the cybersecurity domain, which has both defensive and offensive applications
Development comes as the US has been restricting AI exports to China
Raises strategic concerns for countries including India

stories.headline: Google Caps Meta's Use of Gemini AI Amid Capacity Crunch, Hitting Meta's AI Operations
stories.what: Google has placed limits on Meta's access to its Gemini AI models, according to reports from the Financial Times and picked up by Bloomberg, NDTV Profit, and Firstpost. The restrictions stem from a capacity shortfall on Google's end, meaning Meta — which had been relying on Gemini for certain AI operations — now faces constrained access to one of the most powerful AI platforms available. The development highlights the supply-side pressures building across the frontier AI infrastructure ecosystem.
stories.lens: When two of the world's largest tech companies hit capacity limits on AI infrastructure, it signals just how strained the global AI compute ecosystem is — with direct implications for Indian startups and enterprises that depend on the same cloud AI services.
stories.article:
Google has moved to limit Meta's access to its Gemini AI models due to a capacity shortfall, in a development that exposes the infrastructure bottlenecks now straining even the largest players in the AI industry. The Financial Times first reported the restriction, with Bloomberg and multiple Indian outlets confirming that Google has effectively put a cap on how much of Gemini's capacity Meta can consume.

For Meta, the constraints are operationally significant. The company has been integrating Gemini into various AI workflows, and a sudden cap on access disrupts those operations at a time when Meta is aggressively expanding its own AI product suite — from Meta AI to its Llama model family. Being throttled by a supplier of Google's scale is an unusual and telling sign of how tight AI compute has become.

The episode underlines a structural reality that has been building quietly: demand for frontier AI model access is outpacing even Google's considerable infrastructure capacity. As hyperscalers race to expand data centres and GPU clusters, the gap between demand and supply is creating friction across the industry — friction that is now visible at the highest levels of Big Tech.

For Indian enterprises and developers who use Google Cloud's AI services or rely on model APIs, this serves as a reminder that AI infrastructure is not an unlimited resource. Capacity crunches at the top of the stack can cascade downward, affecting availability, pricing, and reliability for smaller users further down the chain.
key_facts: Google has capped Meta's access to Gemini AI models
Reason cited is a capacity shortfall on Google's side
Financial Times first reported the restriction
Meta's AI operations are affected by the limited Gemini access
stories.image_credit: The Pancake of Heaven! / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Series: Meet Sol, Terra and Luna — But Access Remains Limited
stories.what: OpenAI has launched the GPT-5.6 series, comprising three new AI models named Sol, Terra, and Luna. Sol, the first to roll out in limited form, features a new reasoning mode and enhanced safety features. Despite the announcement generating significant buzz, the vast majority of users cannot access the models yet, with OpenAI restricting availability as it manages a phased rollout. The launch marks another leap in the rapid cadence of frontier AI model releases.
stories.lens: For Indian developers, startups and students betting on OpenAI tools, knowing which new models are accessible — and when — directly affects what they can build right now.
stories.article:
OpenAI has unveiled its GPT-5.6 series, introducing three distinct AI models — Sol, Terra, and Luna — in what amounts to one of its most ambitious multi-model launches to date. Sol, the first model to see a limited rollout, comes equipped with a new reasoning mode and enhanced safety features, signalling OpenAI's continued push to make its frontier models both more capable and more responsible.

Despite the fanfare, access to all three models remains tightly restricted. The Indian Express reported that almost no one can use them yet, with OpenAI managing a careful, staged release rather than an open launch. Moneycontrol noted that Sol's debut also includes two additional AI capabilities, though the full scope of features is being introduced gradually.

The GPT-5.6 series represents a significant architectural moment for OpenAI: rather than a single flagship update, the company is segmenting its offering into specialised models — a strategy that mirrors how cloud providers tier compute and services. Sol appears positioned as the reasoning-heavy option, while Terra and Luna's distinct roles are expected to become clearer as access widens.

For the Indian AI ecosystem — which encompasses a fast-growing developer community, enterprise adopters, and a wave of AI-native startups — the staggered rollout means that planning around these tools requires patience. The models' eventual wide release will be closely watched, both for their raw capability and for how OpenAI prices and distributes access in markets like India.
key_facts: GPT-5.6 series includes three models: Sol, Terra, and Luna
Sol features a new reasoning mode and enhanced safety features
Rollout is limited — most users cannot access any of the three models yet
OpenAI is managing a phased release rather than an open launch

stories.headline: Anthropic Resumes Limited Mythos 5 Rollout After US Eases Export Restrictions
stories.what: Anthropic has resumed a limited rollout of its Mythos 5 AI model following a relaxation of United States export restrictions that had previously paused the release. The restart signals that regulatory headwinds that had held back the model's wider availability are beginning to ease, at least in a limited capacity. Mythos 5 had been positioned as a significant advance in Anthropic's model lineup, and the resumption of its rollout is being closely watched by developers and enterprises globally, including in India.
stories.lens: For Indian businesses and developers relying on Anthropic's API for AI applications, the easing of US restrictions and Mythos 5's limited return to rollout directly affects what cutting-edge tools they can soon access.
stories.article:
Anthropic has restarted the limited rollout of its Mythos 5 AI model after the United States government eased the export restrictions that had previously brought the release to a halt. The Indian Express reported the development, noting that the resumption remains limited in scope — a careful re-entry rather than a full public launch.

The export controls had effectively frozen wider access to Mythos 5, reflecting the increasingly geopolitical nature of frontier AI deployment. The US government's decision to relax those restrictions, even partially, marks a meaningful shift in the regulatory environment around advanced AI model distribution.

The timing of the resumption also intersects with a competitive context: China's development of AI cybersecurity tools described as being on par with Anthropic's Mythos underscores that the global race in frontier AI is intensifying. For Anthropic, getting Mythos 5 back into the hands of developers — even in limited numbers — is both a commercial and strategic priority.

For India's rapidly expanding AI developer and enterprise community, the eased restrictions and limited rollout offer a cautious signal that access could broaden further. Indian firms that have been evaluating Anthropic's models for large-scale deployments in finance, healthcare, and software development will be watching the rollout trajectory closely before committing to integration plans.
key_facts: Anthropic has resumed a limited rollout of Mythos 5
Resumption follows US easing of export restrictions that had paused the release
Rollout remains limited, not a full public launch
China has reportedly developed an AI tool described as on par with Mythos

stories.headline: OpenAI Unveils Sol, Terra and Luna AI Models — US Government Blocks Wide Release
stories.what: OpenAI has announced three new AI models named Sol, Terra and Luna, but the US government has stepped in to block their wide public release. The move follows a pattern of Washington scrutinising powerful AI systems before permitting broad deployment. The intervention signals that at least some of OpenAI's latest models were deemed significant enough to warrant government oversight before reaching the general public. OpenAI had reportedly briefed US authorities on the models' capabilities ahead of the announcement.
stories.lens: OpenAI's models underpin AI tools used by millions of Indians — any US government restriction on their rollout directly shapes what AI capabilities are accessible in India and when.
stories.article:
OpenAI has unveiled three new artificial intelligence models — Sol, Terra and Luna — but the US government has moved to block their wide release, India Today reported on Thursday. The announcement marks one of the more striking instances of Washington exercising direct control over the public deployment of frontier AI, intervening before the models could reach a broad user base.

The development comes amid a broader moment of reckoning in the US over how to handle increasingly powerful AI systems. NDTV earlier reported that OpenAI's new AI was considered so powerful that the company had to brief the US government before its release — suggesting the Sol, Terra and Luna models, or a closely related system, triggered heightened regulatory attention. The government's decision to block wide release while permitting a limited or staged rollout reflects the emerging framework Washington is applying to the most capable AI systems.

For OpenAI, the intervention is a significant moment. The company has long maintained a complex relationship with policymakers, positioning itself as a safety-conscious developer while racing to the frontier of AI capability. Having the government constrain a product launch — even partially — represents a new kind of check on the industry's pace.

For users and developers globally, including in India, the practical consequence is a delay in accessing what may be among the most capable AI models yet released. The extent of the restrictions, and the timeline for any broader rollout, remained unclear as of Thursday.
key_facts: OpenAI announces three new AI models: Sol, Terra and Luna
US government blocks wide public release of the models
OpenAI reportedly briefed US authorities before release due to models' power
Restricted rollout rather than open availability

stories.headline: FIFA World Cup 2026: latest scores and full results
stories.what: The latest FIFA World Cup 2026 results in full — scorelines, goalscorers and what's coming up next, all in IST.
stories.lens: Every matchday, tracked in your time zone.
stories.article:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 group stage continued with another full matchday. Here are the latest results in full:

South Africa 0-1 Canada. Scorers: Stephan Eustáquio 90+2'. Half-time: 0-0. Venue: Los Angeles (Inglewood).

Algeria 3-3 Austria (Group J). Scorers: Rafik Belghali 45', Riyad Mahrez 60', Riyad Mahrez 90+3', Marko Arnautovic 28', Marcel Sabitzer 55', Sasa Kalajdzic 90+6'. Half-time: 1-1. Venue: Kansas City.
